_meta.authoringNotes: Prevalence and morphology data per Register et al. AJSM 2012 (PMID 22593198) MRI prevalence in asymptomatic adults, and Lee et al. JISAKOS 2015. FAI association per Ganz et al. CORR 2003 (PMID 14646708) and the Warwick Agreement on FAI syndrome (Br J Sports Med 2016, PMID 27629403). Surgical outcomes per Mansor et al. AJSM 2018, Bedi et al. AJSM 2013 (PMID 23449836). Conservative care per FASHIoN/UK FASHIoN trial Griffin et al. Lancet 2018 (PMID 29776815). ICD-10 S73.191A acetabular labrum tear.

h1Title: Hip Labral Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A hip labral tear is a structural injury of the acetabular labrum, the fibrocartilaginous rim that seals the femoral head inside the hip socket and provides roughly 22% of the joint's articular surface area. MRI studies of asymptomatic adults show labral abnormalities in 38-69% of hips by age 40, but tears become symptomatic when paired with bony impingement, dysplasia, or athletic loading. Patients describe a deep, anterior groin pain, often accompanied by clicking, catching, or a sensation of giving way during pivoting or prolonged sitting. Untreated labral tears in the setting of femoroacetabular impingement progress to articular cartilage damage and accelerated hip osteoarthritis within 5-15 years. Treatment ranges from activity modification and targeted physical therapy to hip arthroscopic labral repair, with reattachment outcomes exceeding 80% return-to-sport at two years in athletes under 40.

keyStats.prevalence: MRI prevalence of labral abnormalities 38-69% in asymptomatic adults over 40 (Register 2012); symptomatic prevalence estimated 1-2% of general adult population
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio approximately 2:1 in surgical series; bimodal incidence peaks at 20-30 years (athletes) and 40-55 years (degenerative)
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at hip arthroscopy 28-35 years in athletic populations; 45-55 years for degenerative tears
keyStats.globalCases: Hip arthroscopy volumes have grown 25-fold in the US between 1999 and 2017, exceeding 70,000 procedures per year (JBJS 2019)

definition: A hip labral tear (ICD-10: S73.191A acute; M24.85 chronic) is a partial or complete disruption of the acetabular labrum, a triangular ring of dense fibrocartilage attached to the bony rim of the acetabulum. The labrum extends socket depth, maintains negative intra-articular pressure (the suction-seal effect), distributes synovial fluid for cartilage nutrition, and resists femoral head subluxation. Tears are classified by location (anterosuperior most common at 80-95%, then posterior), morphology (fraying, radial flap, longitudinal peripheral, unstable bucket-handle), and etiology (femoroacetabular impingement, dysplasia, trauma, capsular laxity, degeneration). The anterosuperior labrum is the workhorse region during hip flexion and internal rotation; cam-type femoroacetabular impingement progressively shears this segment with each pivot. Adjacent articular cartilage damage is present in 50-70% of cases at arthroscopy, and the labrum-chondral junction is the most frequent site of associated lesions. Hip labral tears sit at the crossroads of orthopedic sports medicine, hip preservation surgery, physical therapy, and, in advanced disease, total joint arthroplasty.

typesClassification.description: The dominant mechanism in young adults. Cam (aspherical femoral head-neck junction) or pincer (overcoverage of the acetabulum) morphology produces repetitive impingement against the labrum during flexion and internal rotation, causing anterosuperior tearing and adjacent cartilage delamination over years.

In acetabular dysplasia the under-covered femoral head transfers excess load to the anterosuperior labrum, which hypertrophies and ultimately tears. These tears are commonly hypertrophied bucket-handle types and require correction of the underlying dysplasia (periacetabular osteotomy) rather than isolated arthroscopy.

Acute injury from a direct fall on the hip, motor-vehicle posterior dislocation, pivoting injury, or hip subluxation in contact sport. Often a clean radial or peripheral longitudinal tear with adjacent chondral injury.
Wear pattern in middle-aged and older adults; the labrum becomes frayed, thinned, and ossified at the rim. These tears coexist with diffuse osteoarthritis and respond poorly to arthroscopy alone.

Generalized ligamentous laxity, repetitive end-range external rotation (dance, gymnastics, golf), or prior capsulotomy can produce posterior or panlabral tears from subtle anterior femoral head translation.

primarySymptoms: Deep, anterior groin pain that the patient often localizes by cupping the hip with the thumb anteriorly and the fingers over the greater trochanter — the classic C-sign — and which worsens with prolonged sitting, driving, or pivoting.
Mechanical clicking, catching, locking, or a sensation of the hip giving way during rotation, climbing stairs, or rising from a low chair, present in 50-70% of patients with confirmed tears.
Sharp pain reproduced by hip flexion to 90 degrees combined with adduction and internal rotation — the anterior impingement (FADIR) maneuver.
Lateral hip and buttock pain referred along the trochanter and into the proximal posterior thigh, often misattributed to trochanteric bursitis.
Stiffness and pain after long periods of inactivity, with patients reporting they have to walk off the hip for the first 5-10 minutes in the morning.
Limited squat depth, inability to sit cross-legged comfortably, and pain getting in and out of a car — daily activities that load the hip in flexion and rotation.
Symptoms aggravated by impact loading (running, jumping, cutting sports) and partially relieved by rest; persistence of mild groin ache between training sessions.
Sensation of an audible or palpable pop with specific rotational movements, often reproducible on demand by the patient.
earlyWarningSigns: Activity-related groin pain in a young athlete that resolves with rest but recurs reliably with sport — particularly in soccer, hockey, ballet, martial arts, and golf
New difficulty sitting cross-legged on the floor or putting on socks and shoes
Subtle catching or clicking in the hip without yet any pain, often reported during routine sport physicals
Limited internal rotation of the affected hip at 90 degrees of flexion on exam (less than 20 degrees)
Persistent unilateral hip stiffness in the morning lasting more than 15 minutes in an otherwise healthy adult under 40
emergencySigns: Sudden inability to bear weight on the hip after a fall, motor-vehicle collision, or sports injury — exclude femoral neck fracture or posterior dislocation with urgent radiographs
Acute severe hip pain with shortened, externally rotated lower limb — femoral neck fracture or hip dislocation
Hip pain accompanied by fever, chills, and inability to tolerate any joint motion — exclude septic arthritis of the hip
Progressive loss of sensation in the groin, perineum, or saddle distribution following trauma — exclude associated lumbosacral injury
Hip pain with rapidly enlarging thigh mass, escalating night pain, or unintentional weight loss — exclude underlying bone or soft-tissue tumor

causes.description: Bony abnormality at the femoral head-neck junction (cam) or acetabular rim overcoverage (pincer) drives repetitive shear at the anterosuperior labrum during hip flexion and internal rotation. FAI morphology is present in 50-70% of patients undergoing labral repair and is the dominant etiology in adults under 40.

A shallow or under-covered acetabulum (lateral center-edge angle below 25 degrees) transfers force to a hypertrophied labrum that bears load it was not designed to carry. The labrum eventually fails as a bucket-handle or peripheral tear. Isolated labral repair without correcting dysplasia has high failure rates.

Direct fall on the hip, posterior dislocation from a dashboard injury, pivoting injuries in contact sports (American football, rugby, soccer), or hip subluxation in ballet and gymnastics can produce clean tears. Posterior tears are over-represented in trauma compared to FAI.

Sports and occupations that demand extreme rotation or flexion — ballet, gymnastics, ice hockey goaltending, martial arts kicking, golf, soccer — generate cumulative microtrauma even in normal-shaped hips. Female ballet dancers show labral pathology rates above 50% on MRI screening.

causes.cause: Generalized ligamentous laxity and capsular insufficiency
causes.description: Beighton score above 4 or connective-tissue conditions (Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan) predispose to hip microinstability. Subtle anterior translation of the femoral head stresses the anterior labrum. Prior hip arthroscopy with unrepaired capsulotomy is another iatrogenic cause.

Age-related fibrocartilage degeneration produces fraying, thinning, and ossification of the labrum. By age 60 over 60% of MRIs show labral abnormalities. These tears usually coexist with chondral wear and progress to symptomatic osteoarthritis.

riskFactors.description: Women constitute 55-75% of hip arthroscopy populations. Lower femoral neck-shaft angle, increased femoral anteversion, and higher rates of dysplasia and laxity all contribute to higher symptomatic tear rates.

Soccer, ice hockey, American football, rugby, basketball, lacrosse, ballet, gymnastics, golf, and martial arts impose repetitive flexion-rotation loads. Professional hockey players show cam morphology rates above 70% on imaging.

Inherited and developmental femoral head-neck and acetabular shape determine impingement risk. Cam morphology develops during skeletal maturation and is more common in males; pincer morphology is more common in females.
Developmental dysplasia of the hip persists into adulthood in roughly 1-2% of the population. Lateral center-edge angle below 25 degrees predicts symptomatic labral failure.
Connective-tissue laxity allows microinstability and stresses the labrum. Patients with Ehlers-Danlos and similar conditions have higher labral-tear prevalence and lower surgical success rates.
Prior posterior hip dislocation or subluxation, even after radiographic reduction, carries a 30-60% risk of associated labral or chondral injury.
Bimodal symptomatic incidence: young athletes 20-30 years and middle-aged adults 40-55 years. Younger patients tear from FAI; older patients combine degenerative tears with early osteoarthritis.

affectedDemographics: United States: hip arthroscopy volumes exceed 70,000 procedures per year, growing from under 3,000 in 1999 (JBJS 2019). Patients average 35-40 years; women constitute 55-65% of cases.
Athletic populations: cam morphology prevalence reaches 70-90% in professional hockey and football players; symptomatic labral tear rates 5-15% of active rosters.
Female ballet dancers: MRI labral pathology over 50% in elite dancers; dysplasia and hypermobility both contribute.
Older adults: degenerative labral tears coexist with osteoarthritis; MRI labral abnormality prevalence rises from 38% age 18-40 to over 90% above age 60 (Register 2012).
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines history, provocative physical examination, plain radiographs to assess bony morphology, and MRI arthrography for direct visualization of the labrum. History elicits the C-sign, mechanical symptoms, sport or work demands, and prior trauma. Examination focuses on hip range of motion (loss of internal rotation at 90 degrees flexion is the single most sensitive finding for FAI-associated tears) and provocative tests — the FADIR (Flexion-Adduction-Internal-Rotation) maneuver has sensitivity above 85% but specificity below 50%, while the FABER (Flexion-Abduction-External-Rotation) and posterior impingement tests help localize. Plain anteroposterior pelvis and Dunn lateral radiographs assess cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees), pincer morphology (cross-over sign, lateral center-edge angle >40), and dysplasia (LCEA <25). MR arthrography with intra-articular gadolinium is the imaging gold standard with sensitivity 80-90% and specificity 50-90% for labral tears, far higher than non-arthrographic MRI. A diagnostic intra-articular anesthetic injection (lidocaine ± steroid) is decisive when imaging is equivocal: pain relief above 50% confirms intra-articular origin. Direct arthroscopic visualization remains the definitive reference standard but is not used for diagnosis alone. Differential diagnosis must exclude lumbar radiculopathy, athletic pubalgia (sports hernia), iliopsoas tendinopathy, adductor strain, trochanteric bursitis, and stress fracture of the femoral neck.

diagnosticTests.test: Standing AP pelvis and Dunn lateral hip radiographs
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cam morphology, pincer overcoverage, dysplasia, joint-space narrowing, and incidental tumors
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three radiographs taken standing; total exposure under one minute. Results read same day. Critical first study before MRI.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the labrum, articular cartilage, and capsule; identifies tear location, morphology, and adjacent chondral damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sterile injection of dilute gadolinium into the hip joint under fluoroscopy or ultrasound (5-10 minutes), then 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner. Mild post-procedure soreness for 24-48 hours. Results typically available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms intra-articular source of pain when imaging is equivocal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Image-guided injection of lidocaine into the hip joint, performed in 10-15 minutes. Pain diary kept over the following 4-24 hours; over 50% pain reduction is considered a positive diagnostic response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces impingement pain and assesses the anterior and posterior hip
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examiner moves the hip into specific positions on the table. Brief sharp pain reproduction is the positive finding. No imaging or invasive component.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies loss of internal rotation at 90 degrees flexion and identifies dysplastic over-rotation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Five minutes on the exam table. Internal rotation below 20 degrees at 90 flexion is highly suspicious for FAI-related labral pathology.

diagnosticTests.test: 3D CT of the proximal femur and acetabulum
diagnosticTests.purpose: Pre-surgical planning of cam and pincer resection; quantifies femoral version and acetabular version
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CT scan, 10-15 minutes. Three-dimensional reconstructions calculated by the radiology workstation. Used selectively for complex morphology or revision cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for confirming labral tear morphology, chondral status, and capsular integrity at the time of repair
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general or spinal anesthesia in the operating room, typically as part of definitive repair rather than diagnosis alone.

treatmentOverview: Management is staged: virtually every patient starts with 6-12 weeks of structured non-operative care. This includes activity modification (limit deep flexion, pivoting, and prolonged sitting), 8-12 weeks of physical therapy focused on hip stabilizer strength (gluteus medius and maximus, deep external rotators, core), motor-control retraining, and selective NSAIDs. The 2018 UK FASHIoN trial showed that arthroscopic surgery produced clinically meaningful additional benefit over personalized hip therapy at 12 months in FAI syndrome (mean iHOT-33 difference 6.8 points, p<0.001), but a substantial subset of patients improve with conservative care alone. Surgical candidates are those with mechanical symptoms, persistent pain despite physical therapy, identifiable structural pathology on imaging, joint space preserved (Tönnis grade 0-1), and a positive diagnostic injection. Hip arthroscopy has evolved to labral repair (suture-anchor reattachment to the acetabular rim) rather than debridement whenever the labral tissue is salvageable, with concomitant treatment of underlying FAI (femoral osteochondroplasty for cam, acetabular rim trim for pincer). Capsular closure preserves stability. Labral reconstruction with autograft or allograft is performed when tissue is non-viable. In dysplasia, periacetabular osteotomy redirects the acetabulum and is the definitive operation; isolated labral repair fails without bony correction. In advanced osteoarthritis (Tönnis grade ≥2), arthroscopy outcomes deteriorate and total hip arthroplasty is considered. Postoperative rehabilitation spans 4-6 months for return to running and 6-9 months for return to cutting sports.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and load management
medicalTreatments.description: Avoid deep hip flexion, sustained sitting, pivoting, and end-range rotation for 6-12 weeks. Replace running and cutting sports with cycling, elliptical, or swimming. Often combined with NSAIDs and PT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-50% of patients with FAI-related tears achieve durable symptom control without surgery at 12-24 months (FASHIoN, FIRST trials)

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physical therapy (8-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Targets gluteus medius and maximus strength, deep hip external rotator endurance, lumbopelvic control, and movement re-education. Personalised hip therapy protocol underpins the conservative arm of FASHIoN and FIRST.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mean iHOT-33 improvement 15-20 points at 8-12 months; 40-50% avoid surgery at 2 years

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 8 hours, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral anti-inflammatories for symptomatic pain control during the rehabilitation phase. Topical diclofenac is an alternative with lower systemic exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 20-30% at 4 weeks in cohort studies; no disease-modifying effect

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided injection used selectively for diagnosis and for symptomatic flares not responding to oral therapy. Limited to no more than 2-3 per year due to chondrotoxicity concerns.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 40-60% at 4-12 weeks in roughly half of patients; effect typically wanes by 3-6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular platelet-rich plasma (PRP)
medicalTreatments.description: Concentrated autologous platelet injection investigated for symptomatic relief in FAI-related labral tears. Evidence remains limited; selected sports-medicine practices use it in younger patients seeking to delay surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Small RCTs show pain reduction of 20-30% at 6 months versus hyaluronic acid or saline; no convincing structural healing

medicalTreatments.name: Acetaminophen (paracetamol 500-1000 mg every 6 hours, max 3 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Background analgesic for patients intolerant of NSAIDs or with cardiovascular or renal contraindications. Used alongside PT during the conservative trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain reduction; safer profile than long-course NSAIDs

surgicalOptions.name: Hip arthroscopy with labral repair and FAI correction (femoral osteochondroplasty ± acetabular rim trim, with capsular closure)
surgicalOptions.description: Gold-standard hip-preservation procedure for symptomatic FAI-related labral tears in patients with preserved joint space. Suture anchors reattach the labrum to the acetabular rim while bony abnormalities are reshaped. Capsular closure preserves stability.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to sport 75-85% at 24 months in athletes under 40; iHOT-33 improvement of 25-35 points at 2 years; 10-year survivorship free of conversion to arthroplasty 75-85%

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic labral debridement (selective)
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of a frayed, irreparable portion of the labrum. Reserved for older patients with limited tissue quality. Replaced by repair in most modern series.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 60-70% at 2 years but inferior long-term durability versus repair; faster progression to osteoarthritis

surgicalOptions.name: Labral reconstruction with autograft or allograft
surgicalOptions.description: Used when the native labrum is unreconstructible (ossified, hypoplastic, or extensively damaged). Iliotibial band autograft or hamstring/allograft tissue is sutured into the rim to recreate the suction seal.
surgicalOptions.successRate: iHOT-33 improvement comparable to repair in selected revision cases; conversion to arthroplasty 8-12% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Periacetabular osteotomy (PAO)
surgicalOptions.description: Open hip-preservation operation for symptomatic acetabular dysplasia. The acetabulum is freed and rotated to provide adequate femoral head coverage. Concurrent or staged labral repair addresses associated tear.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hip survivorship without arthroplasty 70-85% at 15 years in adults under 40; substantial improvement in mWOMAC and UCLA activity scores

surgicalOptions.name: Total hip arthroplasty (THA)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for end-stage hip osteoarthritis when labral disease has progressed to Tönnis grade 3 or articular cartilage loss is diffuse. Modern implants and bearings deliver excellent outcomes across decades.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 20-year implant survivorship 85-95% with modern bearings; patient-reported satisfaction above 90% at 1 year

alternativeTreatments.name: Intra-articular platelet-rich plasma (PRP)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used adjunctively in some sports medicine practices to delay surgery in younger athletes. RCT data are limited; modest short-term pain benefit reported.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pilates and Feldenkrais movement retraining
alternativeTreatments.description: Movement-quality programs that augment formal physical therapy by improving lumbopelvic control and reducing impingement-provoking patterns; observational benefit in dancers and golfers.

whySeeSpecialist: Hip labral tears benefit from evaluation by a sports medicine or hip-preservation orthopedic surgeon experienced in hip arthroscopy, FAI assessment, and periacetabular osteotomy. Specialist referral is required when symptoms persist beyond 8-12 weeks of structured physical therapy, when imaging shows correctable bony morphology, or when mechanical symptoms (locking, true giving way) are present.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a fellowship-trained hip-preservation or sports-medicine orthopedic surgeon performing at least 50 hip arthroscopies annually. Ask about labral repair versus debridement rate (modern centers repair more than 85%), capsular closure technique, and indications for PAO over arthroscopy. Centers with a hip-preservation program offering both arthroscopy and open hip surgery provide the broadest options.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated sports medicine or hip-preservation service
Surgeons performing >50 hip arthroscopies per year
Access to MR arthrography and 3D CT planning
Image-guided injection capability for diagnostic blocks
Onsite physical therapy with hip-specific post-arthroscopy protocols
keyFacilities: Physical therapy clinics with sports rehab specialization
preventionStrategies: Maintain hip mobility through dynamic warm-up before sport, particularly in flexion-rotation drills.
Strengthen gluteus medius, gluteus maximus, and deep external rotators to support hip neuromotor control.
Avoid forced end-range hip motion (ballistic stretching, deep squats below 90 degrees with poor form) in adolescents with closing growth plates, when cam morphology develops.
Address asymmetric core and pelvic-floor weakness in pivoting athletes early.
Manage body weight to within healthy BMI range — each unit of BMI above 25 increases joint reaction force during gait.
Modify high-risk activities (deep squat occupations, ballistic golf swing positions) if early symptoms emerge.
lifestyleModifications: Replace running and cutting sports with cycling (seat height up), elliptical, or pool-based exercise during symptomatic phases.
Use a lumbar pillow and avoid soft, low chairs; aim for hips slightly above knees when sitting.
Stand and walk briefly every 30-45 minutes during desk work or long drives.
Sleep with a pillow between the knees to neutralize hip rotation.
Resume cutting and pivoting sports only after completing a sport-specific functional progression cleared by a sports physio.
Use a hot pack before activity and ice after if there is post-activity soreness.

recommended: Omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) which may modestly reduce joint inflammation
Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day) during postoperative rehabilitation to support muscle recovery
Vitamin D and calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) for bone health
Anti-inflammatory plant-rich diet emphasizing vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes
avoid: Ultra-processed foods and added sugars that may amplify systemic inflammation
Excess alcohol, which impairs muscle recovery and tendon healing
Smoking, which raises postoperative complication and labral healing failure rates
Crash dieting during rehabilitation that compromises protein intake and recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Cardiovascular fitness is maintained with low-impact options: stationary cycling with the seat slightly raised, elliptical, deep-water running, and swimming with a pull buoy (avoid breaststroke kick). Resistance training focuses on glute medius, glute maximus, deep external rotators, core, and quadriceps; avoid deep hip flexion exercises (deep squats, leg presses below 90, hip flexor loading) during acute flares. Sport-specific return follows a graded program over 4-6 months postoperatively for repair patients.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed home exercise program daily — 15-25 minutes of stabilizer and mobility work.
Limit cumulative sitting to under 4 hours per day during symptomatic periods.
Use ice 15-20 minutes after activity if soreness emerges.
Track pain on a 0-10 scale and avoid activities that produce ≥3/10 pain during the rehab phase.
Take NSAIDs only with food and water; discontinue if gastrointestinal symptoms develop.
Attend physical therapy follow-up every 2-4 weeks during the first 12 weeks of conservative care.
prognosis: Outlook depends on age, joint space at presentation, the underlying morphology, and tissue quality at surgery. Athletes under 40 with preserved joint space, FAI-driven tears, and salvageable labral tissue achieve return-to-sport in 75-85% at two years after labral repair, with mean iHOT-33 improvements of 25-35 points. Ten-year survivorship free of conversion to total hip arthroplasty after primary arthroscopic labral repair and FAI correction is 75-85% in modern series. Patients with Tönnis grade 2 or worse at the time of arthroscopy, age above 50, or BMI above 30 have substantially lower outcomes and higher conversion rates to arthroplasty. Dysplastic hips treated with isolated arthroscopy without periacetabular osteotomy have early failure rates approaching 30-50%, while combined or staged PAO with labral repair produces durable outcomes. Most patients diagnosed early and managed appropriately maintain active recreational sport, though high-level professional careers in cutting sports are at risk when cam morphology is severe.
recoveryTimeline: After arthroscopic labral repair: crutches and partial weight-bearing 2-4 weeks; return to desk work in 7-14 days; pain-free walking by 6-8 weeks; stationary cycling at 4 weeks; running progression at 12-16 weeks; agility and cutting at 16-20 weeks; full return to sport by 4-6 months for non-cutting sports and 6-9 months for high-cutting sports. Conservative management improves over 8-12 weeks with structured therapy.
complications: Progression to symptomatic hip osteoarthritis — annual functional review and consideration of total hip arthroplasty when joint space narrows below 2 mm.
Postoperative femoral neck stress fracture (under 1% but devastating) — driven by aggressive femoral osteochondroplasty in poor bone; managed with protected weight-bearing or fixation.
Heterotopic ossification after hip arthroscopy (5-30% radiographic, 1-5% symptomatic) — prophylactic indomethacin 75 mg daily for 7 days reduces incidence.
Iatrogenic chondral injury or labral suture pullout — diagnosed by persistent or recurrent mechanical symptoms; revision arthroscopy may be required.
Lateral femoral cutaneous nerve neuropraxia from portal traction — typically transient (3-12 weeks).
Capsular insufficiency and microinstability — modern technique closes the capsule routinely to mitigate this risk.

supportResources.name: International Society for Hip Arthroscopy (ISHA)
supportResources.description: Professional society for hip-preservation surgeons with patient-facing resources on FAI and labral disease.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Hip Labral Tear
supportResources.description: Patient education on FAI and labral pathology from the AAOS.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Society for Sports Medicine (AOSSM)
supportResources.description: Sports-medicine specialty resources for athletes with hip injuries.

supportResources.name: British Hip Society
supportResources.description: UK specialty body with patient and surgeon guidance on hip preservation and arthroplasty.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hip Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hip osteoarthritis presents with progressive groin and lateral pain, stiffness, and joint-space narrowing on radiographs in middle-aged and older adults. Labral tears in younger adults predate measurable joint-space loss and show structural labral defects on MR arthrogram without diffuse cartilage thinning.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sciatica / Lumbar Radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatica produces buttock and posterior leg pain radiating below the knee with dermatomal sensory loss and positive straight-leg raise. Hip labral tear pain is anterior groin (C-sign), reproduced by FADIR, and relieved by intra-articular anesthetic injection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adductor Strain / Groin Pull
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adductor strains produce medial groin pain on resisted adduction with palpable adductor tenderness and respond to rest and progressive loading. Labral tears produce deeper anterior groin pain reproduced by hip rotation rather than adduction, with mechanical symptoms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trochanteric Bursitis (Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trochanteric bursitis produces lateral hip pain reproduced by direct palpation of the greater trochanter and pain lying on the affected side. Labral tear pain is anterior, deeper, and reproduced by FADIR rather than direct palpation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hip Abductor Tendinopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy mimics trochanteric pain syndromes with lateral hip ache, Trendelenburg gait, and weakness on resisted abduction. Labral pathology produces groin-dominant pain with rotation provocation.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated labral tears with FAI accelerate hip osteoarthritis; hip preservation surgery aims to delay or prevent this progression.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another structural sports-medicine entity sharing diagnostic principles (MR arthrography, provocative testing, staged conservative-then-surgical management).

relatedConditions.name: Sciatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Common differential and frequent coexisting cause of buttock and posterior thigh pain that complicates hip evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Groin Pull
relatedConditions.relevance: Adductor strain frequently coexists with labral pathology in pivoting athletes and is the leading mimic of anterior groin pain.

faqs.question: What is a hip labral tear?
faqs.answer: A hip labral tear is a structural injury to the acetabular labrum, a ring of fibrocartilage around the hip socket that seals the joint and provides stability. Tears most often occur at the anterosuperior labrum and produce deep groin pain, clicking, and catching, especially during pivoting or prolonged sitting.

faqs.question: What does a hip labral tear feel like?
faqs.answer: Patients describe deep anterior groin pain, often demonstrating the C-sign by cupping the hip with thumb in front and fingers over the side. Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, driving, pivoting, and getting in and out of a car. Mechanical clicking, catching, or giving way is reported in 50-70% of confirmed tears.

faqs.question: How is a hip labral tear diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines history, FADIR provocative exam, plain radiographs (alpha angle, lateral center-edge angle), and MR arthrography with intra-articular gadolinium contrast. A diagnostic intra-articular anesthetic injection that produces >50% pain relief confirms an intra-articular pain source. Direct arthroscopy is the reference standard but used at the time of surgery.

faqs.question: Can a hip labral tear heal on its own?
faqs.answer: The labrum has limited blood supply and most tears do not heal anatomically. However, 30-50% of FAI-related tears become asymptomatic with structured physical therapy and activity modification, achieving durable symptom control without surgery at 12-24 months in the FASHIoN and FIRST trials.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of hip labral repair?
faqs.answer: Modern arthroscopic labral repair with FAI correction yields return to sport in 75-85% of athletes under 40 at 24 months, and 10-year survivorship free of total hip arthroplasty conversion of 75-85%. Outcomes are lower in patients over 50, with joint-space narrowing, or with BMI above 30.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after hip arthroscopy?
faqs.answer: Crutches and partial weight-bearing for 2-4 weeks, desk work within 1-2 weeks, pain-free walking by 6-8 weeks, stationary cycling at 4 weeks, running at 12-16 weeks, and return to sport at 4-9 months depending on sport. Full recovery often takes 9-12 months.

faqs.question: Is hip labral tear surgery worth it?
faqs.answer: In symptomatic FAI syndrome the UK FASHIoN trial showed arthroscopic surgery produced significant additional benefit over physical therapy at 12 months (mean iHOT-33 6.8 points higher, p<0.001). Surgery is most appropriate when conservative care fails, mechanical symptoms persist, joint space is preserved, and a diagnostic injection has been positive.

faqs.question: What happens if a labral tear is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated FAI-associated labral tears progress to articular cartilage damage and accelerated hip osteoarthritis over 5-15 years. Patients with dysplasia plus labral tear are at particular risk of rapid joint deterioration without bony correction (periacetabular osteotomy).

faqs.question: Can I run with a hip labral tear?
faqs.answer: Running often worsens labral pain because of repetitive impact and hip flexion-rotation loading. Many patients can continue running pain-free at lower mileage with strengthening and form modifications, but cutting and pivoting sports usually require surgical correction in athletes who want full return to performance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between FAI and a hip labral tear?
faqs.answer: FAI (femoroacetabular impingement) is the underlying bony morphology — cam or pincer abnormality — that produces repetitive contact between the femoral head-neck junction and the acetabular rim. A labral tear is the consequence of that contact over time. The two conditions usually coexist and are treated together at surgery.

faqs.question: Is a hip labral tear the same as hip osteoarthritis?
faqs.answer: No. A labral tear is a focal structural injury to the fibrocartilaginous rim, while hip osteoarthritis is diffuse loss of articular cartilage across the joint. Untreated labral tears in FAI accelerate osteoarthritis, but they are distinct stages of disease with different treatment approaches.

faqs.question: Does physical therapy work for hip labral tears?
faqs.answer: Yes for many patients. The UK FASHIoN trial showed personalized hip therapy improved mean iHOT-33 scores by 15-20 points at 8-12 months, with 40-50% of patients avoiding surgery at two years. Physical therapy targets gluteal strength, deep external rotators, core control, and motion re-education.

faqs.question: Can a labral tear cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Limited hip motion forces compensatory lumbar movement during sitting, walking, and sport, and many labral tear patients present with combined hip-spine syndrome. Treating the labral tear often improves coexisting low-back pain, but persistent lumbar pathology may require independent assessment.

faqs.question: How long does an MRI of the hip take?
faqs.answer: A standard hip MRI without contrast takes 30-40 minutes. MR arthrography adds 10-15 minutes for image-guided intra-articular gadolinium injection plus the MRI scan, total appointment 60-75 minutes. The arthrogram offers higher diagnostic accuracy for labral tears (sensitivity 80-90%).

faqs.question: What is the C-sign in hip pain?
faqs.answer: The C-sign is the characteristic gesture patients with intra-articular hip pathology make to localize their pain: they cup the affected hip with the thumb posterior to the greater trochanter and the fingers extending into the anterior groin. It indicates deep, joint-line pain rather than superficial bursitis or muscle strain.

faqs.question: Can young athletes get labral tears?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cam-type FAI develops during adolescent skeletal maturation, particularly in male athletes playing soccer, ice hockey, basketball, and American football. Symptomatic labral tears in elite young athletes are common and often present in the second or third decade with deep groin pain and mechanical symptoms.

faqs.question: Are hip labral tears more common in women?
faqs.answer: Women comprise 55-75% of hip arthroscopy populations. Lower femoral neck-shaft angle, increased anteversion, higher rates of acetabular dysplasia, generalized joint laxity, and pincer morphology contribute. Female ballet dancers show labral abnormality rates above 50% on MRI screening.

faqs.question: What activities should I avoid with a hip labral tear?
faqs.answer: Avoid deep hip flexion (deep squats below 90 degrees, sustained sitting in low chairs), pivoting and cutting sports, and forced end-range rotation. Replace running with cycling, elliptical, or swimming during symptomatic phases. Return to sport after a graded sport-specific progression cleared by a sports physiotherapist.

faqs.question: Will a steroid injection cure a labral tear?
faqs.answer: No. Steroid injection does not heal the labrum; it temporarily reduces pain and inflammation, lasting 4-12 weeks in about half of patients. It is most useful diagnostically (lidocaine confirms intra-articular origin) and for managing flares while structured rehabilitation continues.

faqs.question: Can a labral tear cause hip dislocation?
faqs.answer: Isolated labral tears rarely cause overt dislocation but can produce subtle microinstability with sensations of giving way, especially in patients with generalized ligamentous laxity. Frank dislocation requires significant trauma (motor-vehicle accident, sports collision) and is usually associated with chondral and labral injury together.

faqs.question: How much does hip arthroscopy cost?
faqs.answer: In the United States, hip arthroscopy ranges from $15,000-$35,000 in total facility and surgeon charges when paying out-of-pocket, with most commercially insured patients having out-of-pocket costs of $1,500-$5,000. Costs in the UK NHS and most European public systems are fully covered for appropriate indications.

faqs.question: Can hip labral tears be prevented?
faqs.answer: Cam morphology that drives most tears develops during adolescent growth and is partly inherited and partly activity-related. Avoiding excessive forced end-range hip motion during late adolescence may reduce risk in at-risk sports. Maintaining gluteal strength and core control reduces symptomatic onset in adults with FAI morphology.
